description: The currently displayed image is an irrelevant interface button marked with the text 'Check for updates', attached with a decorative icon consisting of a blue-yellow circular ring and a red bookmark-shaped pattern inside. This image has no correlation with the provided research content of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels. No valid material science related information, including microstructure characteristics, phase composition, mechanical performance, processing parameters or experimental results of the studied alloy systems, can be extracted from this image. It is confirmed to be an incorrectly uploaded resource that does not belong to the target paper's figure content.

This figure presents microstructures of four Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels with varied Ni/Cr contents: 3Cr steel, 2Ni steel, 2Ni3Cr steel, and 2Ni5Cr steel. Subfigures (a₁-d₁) are EBSD inverse pole figure maps showing grain orientation distributions, (a₂-d₂) are EBSD phase maps confirming all samples consist of single FCC γ-austenite phase. Subfigures (e-h) are TEM bright-field images showing micrometer-scale grain morphologies, (i-l) are TEM dark-field images from {110} LRO or {110} κ reflections: high-Cr 3Cr/2Ni5Cr steels contain homogeneously distributed nanoscale LRO domains, while high-Ni 2Ni/2Ni3Cr steels contain cuboidal κ-carbides with the largest size (~10 nm) in 2Ni steel. Subfigures (m-p) are selected area electron diffraction patterns along [011] γ zone axis verifying superlattice spots from LRO/κ-carbides. It supports the core conclusion that Ni promotes κ-carbide growth while Cr suppresses κ-carbide and facilitates LRO formation to tune mechanical properties.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of four Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples, designated 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr, all subjected to the same hot rolling and ultra-fast cooling processing. The x-axis records 2θ angles ranging from 30° to 100°, while the y-axis denotes diffraction intensity in arbitrary units (a.u.). All four samples display identical characteristic diffraction peaks assigned to the face-centred cubic (FCC) γ austenite phase, indexed as γ(111), γ(200), γ(220), γ(311), and γ(222). No additional peaks corresponding to secondary phases (such as B2 phase with body-centred cubic structure) are detected, proving all tested steels possess a single γ austenite phase structure. This XRD result is consistent with EBSD analysis findings, validating that the studied Ni and Cr alloying strategy does not introduce unwanted intermetallic secondary phases in this low-density steel system.

This figure characterizes long-range ordered (LRO) domains and κ-carbides in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels with varying Ni and Cr alloying contents. Subfigures (a), (c), (e), (g) are high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images captured along the [011]γ zone axis for 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, 2Ni5Cr steels respectively, each inset with a fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern that shows both matrix and superlattice reflections corresponding to LRO domains or κ-carbides. Subfigures (b), (d), (f), (h) are inverse FFT (IFFT) images generated using {011} and {100} superlattice reflections, highlighting the morphology of nanoscale LRO domains (ellipsoidal, for high Cr steels) or κ-carbides (cuboidal, for high Ni steels), with a 10 nm scale bar provided for each micrograph. Subfigure (i) is a bar chart plotting the average size of these precipitates, measured from over 200 domains: ~10.69 nm for 2Ni steel, ~5.46 nm for 2Ni3Cr steel, ~2.14 nm for 2Ni5Cr steel, and ~1.75 nm for 3Cr steel. The observed trend demonstrates that increasing Cr content reduces the average precipitate size, indicating Cr suppresses the formation and growth of κ-carbides/LRO domains, while Ni promotes κ-carbide precipitation, supporting the alloying synergy effect on microstructure regulation for strength-ductility optimization.

This group of figures presents tensile mechanical properties of four Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels with different Ni/Cr additions: 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, 2Ni5Cr. Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves, (b) shows true stress-strain curves, which demonstrate that 2Ni steel has the highest yield and tensile strength while 3Cr steel has the lowest strength but excellent ductility. Subplot (c) presents strain hardening rate curves, with three distinct stages (I: sharp decrease, II: stable/rising, III: decreasing) for all samples; higher Cr content prolongs stage II and maintains higher hardening rate in stage III, while Ni addition shortens these stages. Subplot (d) compares yield strength (YS) against product of tensile strength and total elongation (TS×TEL) with reported Fe-Al, Fe-Mn, Fe-Mn-C, Fe-Mn-Al-C and stainless steels, showing the Ni/Cr-alloyed steels in this study achieve superior strength-ductility combination with notably higher YS at equivalent TS×TEL values. The core conclusion is that Ni and Cr alloying synergy effectively enhances both strength and ductility of the lightweight austenitic steels.

This figure presents SEM images of fracture surfaces for four ultra-fast cooled Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels with different Ni and Cr alloying additions. Subplot (a) corresponds to 3Cr steel, (b) to 2Ni steel, (c) to 2Ni3Cr steel, and (d) to 2Ni5Cr steel, respectively. Each subplot includes an inset showing the macroscopic morphology of the fracture surface, and the scale bar for all SEM micrographs is 200 μm. Abundant dimples are observed in the fiber zone of all four steel samples, indicating that all investigated steels exhibit a ductile fracture mode. The fracture morphology results are consistent with the measured tensile ductility of the steels, confirming that Ni and Cr alloying synergy can maintain good ductility while enhancing strength of the low-density steels.

This set of TEM images presents the microstructural evolution of 3Cr austenitic low-density steel (Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C-3Cr) under different engineering tensile strains: (a-c) for 3% strain, the main deformation feature is planar dislocation slip within {111} slip bands, including dislocation multipoles and interacting slip bands from different slip systems; (d-f) for 10% strain, the slip band spacing narrows, slip band count and dislocation density increase, multiple slip systems are activated forming intersecting high-density dislocation arrays (HDDA); (g-l) for near-failure (LD, 54% strain) state, high-density dislocations, ultra-fine slip bands, nanoscale deformation twins, and stacking faults are observed. Insets are selected area electron diffraction patterns along the [011]γ zone axis confirming the FCC austenite matrix. The figure supports that deformation of 3Cr steel is dominated by dislocation slip and HDDA, with strain hardening mainly contributed by HDDA densification and dynamic slip band refinement, while rare twins/stacking faults near fracture contribute little to ductility.

This figure displays transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images characterizing the deformation-induced microstructural evolution of 2Ni alloyed Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steel at different engineering strain levels. Subfigures (a, b) correspond to 5% strain, showing initial planar slip bands along {111} traces, with inserted selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns indexed to the [011] zone axis of FCC austenite phase. Subfigures (c-g) correspond to 10% strain, presenting proliferated slip bands with reduced inter-spacing. Subfigures (h-l) correspond to the strain level close to final failure (LD), revealing high-density dislocations, very fine slip bands, and stacking faults at higher magnification. These observations confirm that the deformation mechanism of 2Ni steel is dominated by planar slip, with continuous slip band refinement as strain increases, and a small amount of stacking faults only form at near-failure high strain levels.

This figure shows two transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C-2Ni (2Ni) austenitic low-density steel sampled from the region close to final tensile failure. Subfigure (a) is a dark-field TEM image with a 30 nm scale bar, which clearly reveals the distribution of cuboidal κ-carbide precipitates with bright contrast in the austenite matrix, consistent with the reported average size of ~10.69 nm for κ-carbides in 2Ni steel. Subfigure (b) is a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image with a 10 nm scale bar, demonstrating the lattice structure of κ-carbides and their coherent interface with the austenite matrix. These results verify that κ-carbides remain stable during plastic deformation in Ni-alloyed low-density steels, providing direct microstructural evidence for the precipitation strengthening effect of Ni alloying that accounts for the high yield strength of the 2Ni steel.

This figure shows four crystal structure unit cell models related to Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels, accompanied by a legend: golden spheres represent Fe/Mn, off-white spheres represent Ni, blue spheres represent Al, and gray spheres represent C. Subplot (a) illustrates the disordered γ phase, where Fe/Mn and Al atoms randomly occupy the vertex and face-center positions of the face-centered cubic (FCC) lattice. Subplot (b) illustrates the ordered γ phase, with Al atoms occupying all vertex positions and Fe/Mn occupying all face-center positions of the FCC lattice. Subplot (c) illustrates κ-carbide with a perovskite E2₁ structure, formed when C atoms occupy the body-centered octahedral interstitial sites, with ideal stoichiometry (Fe, Mn)AlC. Subplot (d) illustrates AlNi₃C, a Ni-rich κ-carbide where Ni replaces Fe/Mn at face-center sites. These structures are used for density functional theory (DFT) calculations to analyze the effect of Ni and Cr alloying on κ-carbide stability, supporting the conclusion that Ni reduces κ-carbide formation energy to promote its precipitation, while Cr increases formation energy to suppress its precipitation.

This set of four bar charts illustrates precipitation hardening (y-axis, unit: MPa) as a function of precipitate radius (x-axis, unit: nm) for four Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels: (a) 3Cr, (b) 2Ni, (c) 2Ni3Cr, (d) 2Ni5Cr. Green bars represent order strengthening (σ_order), orange bars represent the sum of coherency strengthening and modulus mismatch strengthening (σ_coh+σ_mod). Red dashed lines in (a) and (d) mark the transition radius, where the dominant strengthening mechanism switches from σ_order to σ_coh+σ_mod. It is observed that σ_coh+σ_mod acts as the dominant contribution to precipitation hardening for all four steels. Only 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels, which contain small-sized long-range ordered nanodomains, show additional σ_order contributions from precipitates smaller than the critical transition radius. The maximum precipitation hardening value reaches ~39 MPa for 2Ni steel, ~28 MPa for 2Ni3Cr steel, ~21 MPa for 2Ni5Cr steel, and ~15 MPa for 3Cr steel. This figure supports the conclusion that precipitate size regulated by Ni and Cr alloying determines the dominant precipitation strengthening mechanism, further affecting the mechanical properties of the studied steels.

This stacked bar chart presents the yield strength decomposition of four Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steel samples (3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, 2Ni5Cr), as well as their experimentally measured yield strength from tensile tests marked by brown spheres. The y-axis represents yield strength in MPa. Each stacked bar consists of four strengthening contribution components: σ₀ (lattice friction stress, light orange), σ_gb (grain boundary strengthening, green), σ_ss (solution strengthening, purple), and σ_prep (precipitation strengthening, light yellow). The calculated total yield strength shows good agreement with experimental values for all samples. The 2Ni sample has the highest yield strength (~1200 MPa), while the 3Cr sample has the lowest (~970 MPa). Solution strengthening is the dominant strengthening mechanism for all samples, followed by precipitation strengthening. The figure confirms the validity of the yield strength superposition model, and supports that Ni and Cr alloying synergistically adjust precipitation strengthening by modifying precipitate size in these steels.

This figure combines strain hardening rate versus true strain curves and schematic diagrams of deformation microstructure evolution for Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C based 3Cr and 2Ni austenitic low-density steels at different strain stages. The dark blue curve corresponds to 3Cr steel, with a peak strain hardening rate of ~2100 MPa and failure true strain of ~0.4, while the light blue curve corresponds to 2Ni steel, with a peak strain hardening rate of ~1450 MPa and failure true strain of ~0.25. Subplots (a-c) show microstructures of 3Cr steel at 3% strain, 10% strain, and near fracture (LD) respectively, while (d-f) show those of 2Ni steel at 5% strain, 10% strain, and near fracture, with identified features including HDDA, slip bands, deformation twins, and stacking faults. It supports that Cr alloying promotes HDDA formation, slip band refinement, deformation twinning and stacking fault generation, leading to higher strain hardening capability and ductility than 2Ni steel which deforms mainly via planar slip.
